worth_it_explanation: The ads.txt and app-ads.txt files are how a publisher tells buyers which companies are allowed to sell its video and CTV inventory, so a missing or wrong FreeWheel line can cause buyers to treat the supply as unauthorized and bid lower or stop bidding. The check is a read of files the publisher already controls against the seller account FreeWheel assigns, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and any finding points to a specific line a publisher can correct.
intro: Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt), and its app and CTV extension app-ads.txt, is a public file a publisher hosts that lists the companies allowed to sell its inventory and the account each one uses. For a publisher serving video and Connected TV (CTV) ads through FreeWheel, those files are where buyers confirm that FreeWheel is an authorized seller of the publisher's supply. This subcategory looks at whether the FreeWheel entries are present, correct, and current since a wrong or missing line tells the buying side the supply may not be legitimate.

audit_checks.title: The ads.txt or app-ads.txt file is missing the correct FreeWheel seller line
audit_checks.impact_rationale: If a buyer crawls the publisher's ads.txt or app-ads.txt and does not find FreeWheel's domain, the publisher's seller account ID, and a matching DIRECT or RESELLER designation, the buyer treats supply routed through FreeWheel as unauthorized. Many buyers filter unauthorized supply before bidding, so a missing or mismatched line can cut demand and clearing price across the affected inventory rather than on a single placement.

educate.base:
Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt) is a plain-text file a publisher hosts at the root of each domain that lists the companies allowed to sell its inventory. The app and Connected TV (CTV) extension, app-ads.txt, does the same job for inventory distributed through app stores and CTV platforms, where the file is found through the developer website declared in the store listing. Each line names the seller's canonical domain, the publisher's account ID with that seller, a relationship of DIRECT or RESELLER, and an optional certification authority ID. Buyers crawl these files and match the lines against the supply they are offered, so a line is the publisher's public statement that a given seller is allowed to represent its inventory.

For a publisher serving video and CTV ads through FreeWheel, the relevant line names FreeWheel's seller domain, the seller account ID FreeWheel assigns to the publisher, and the relationship. DIRECT means the publisher has a direct account and contract with FreeWheel for that inventory. RESELLER means FreeWheel is authorized to resell the inventory on the publisher's behalf rather than holding the direct relationship. The designation has to match the actual arrangement because buyers read DIRECT and RESELLER differently when they value and verify supply.

Three parts of the line each have to be right for a buyer to treat the supply as authorized:
  • Seller domain: the canonical domain FreeWheel publishes for ads.txt, not a guess or a stale variant
  • Seller account ID: the exact account identifier FreeWheel assigned the publisher, copied without transcription errors
  • Relationship: DIRECT or RESELLER, matching whether the publisher holds the direct account or FreeWheel resells the inventory
When any of the three is missing or wrong, a buyer crawling the file fails to find a match for FreeWheel-routed supply and treats that supply as unauthorized. CTV adds a second failure mode because the file has to be reachable through the developer domain the app store listing points to. If that pointer is wrong or the developer site has no app-ads.txt, buyers cannot find the file at all and the whole app's supply reads as unverified.

You may want to confirm the FreeWheel line exists in every file that should carry it and that all three parts match the values FreeWheel assigned. One approach is to pull the seller domain, account ID, and relationship from the FreeWheel account record, then read the published ads.txt and app-ads.txt files and compare each field against that record.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, the same brand often runs a website, mobile apps, and CTV apps across several platforms, and each distribution surface has its own ads.txt or app-ads.txt requirement. You may want to confirm that the FreeWheel line is present and correct on the website's ads.txt and on the app-ads.txt reached through every app store listing, rather than assuming one correct file covers all of them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce brand running shoppable or product video monetized through FreeWheel, the video may live on the main commerce domain rather than a separate media property. You may want to confirm that the domain hosting the monetized video carries its own ads.txt with the correct FreeWheel line since buyers crawl the domain where the inventory appears, not a parent or marketing domain.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's exact seller domain, the publisher's assigned seller account ID, and the DIRECT or RESELLER guidance for a given account are documented in the FreeWheel partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Read the publisher's published ads.txt and app-ads.txt files and compare the FreeWheel line against the seller domain, account ID, and relationship FreeWheel assigned to the publisher.

browser.label: Web browser
steps: Open the publisher's ads.txt at the root of each web domain, for example 'https://example.com/ads.txt', and find every line whose seller domain belongs to FreeWheel.
For each CTV or mobile app, find the developer website declared in the app store listing, then open 'app-ads.txt' at the root of that developer domain and find the FreeWheel lines.
Compare the seller domain, seller account ID, and DIRECT or RESELLER relationship on each FreeWheel line against the values from the publisher's FreeWheel account record.
Record any file that has no FreeWheel line, any line whose account ID does not match the assigned value, and any line whose relationship does not match the actual arrangement.

ads_txt_validator.label: ads.txt and app-ads.txt validator
steps: Run the publisher's ads.txt and each app-ads.txt URL through a public ads.txt validator to confirm the file parses and the lines are well formed.
Confirm the validator resolves each app-ads.txt through the developer domain the app store listing points to since a broken pointer hides the file from buyers.
Record any parsing error, unreachable file, or malformed FreeWheel line the validator flags.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each domain and app that routes inventory through FreeWheel, whether the published ads.txt or app-ads.txt carries a FreeWheel line and whether its seller domain, account ID, and relationship match the assigned values. A setup where every file that should carry a FreeWheel line does, with all three fields matching, passes. A file that lacks a FreeWheel line, carries a mismatched account ID, or states the wrong relationship is a fail recorded with the file and the field that does not match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the FreeWheel seller domain, account ID, and relationship for each ads.txt and app-ads.txt file above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt or app-ads.txt for {{site_name}} is missing the correct FreeWheel seller line, so buyers crawling the file cannot confirm that FreeWheel is authorized to sell the affected video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory. Many buyers filter supply they read as unauthorized before bidding, so a missing or mismatched line can reduce demand and clearing price across that inventory. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the seller domain, seller account ID, and DIRECT or RESELLER relationship from the FreeWheel account record, then adding or correcting the matching line in every ads.txt and app-ads.txt that should carry it. After the change, recheck each file through a validator to confirm the line is present and well formed.

audit_checks.title: Stale, duplicate, or unauthorized seller lines remain in the file
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Lines for sellers the publisher no longer works with, duplicate FreeWheel entries, or accounts that were never authorized weaken the signal the file is meant to send. Stale and unauthorized lines keep open a path for inventory to be sold under the publisher's name after a relationship ended, and a cluttered file makes it harder for both the publisher and buyers to read which sellers are genuinely current.

educate.base:
An ads.txt or app-ads.txt file is only as trustworthy as it is current. Over time, lines accumulate: a seller relationship ends but the line stays, the same FreeWheel account is pasted twice, or a line is added for an account that was never actually authorized. Each of these weakens the statement the file is meant to make, which is a clean and current list of the sellers allowed to represent the publisher's inventory. The Authorized Digital Sellers standard treats the file as the authoritative declaration, so anything left in it that should not be there is still read by buyers as a live authorization.

Stale lines carry the most risk. When a publisher stops working with a seller, or closes a FreeWheel account tied to inventory it no longer monetizes that way, the line should be removed. A line left in place keeps a path open for that account to sell under the publisher's name, which is the kind of unauthorized sale the standard exists to close. A line for an account the publisher never authorized has the same effect from day one and may point to an entry added in error or by a party that should not have edited the file.

Duplicate lines and formatting clutter are lower-risk but still worth clearing. A FreeWheel line repeated verbatim does no direct harm, but it makes the file harder to read and can hide a second line that differs in a field such as the relationship. Comment lines, blank lines, and notes are allowed by the specification, yet a file that has grown without maintenance becomes hard for the publisher to audit and for buyers to parse with confidence.

A few patterns point to a file that needs cleanup:
  • Lines for sellers or FreeWheel accounts the publisher no longer works with
  • The same FreeWheel account ID appearing on more than one line, especially with different relationships
  • Seller lines the publisher cannot tie to a current, authorized relationship
You may want to reconcile the lines in the file against the publisher's current list of authorized sellers and FreeWheel accounts. One approach is to list every seller line, mark each as current, stale, duplicate, or unrecognized, and remove the lines that are not a current authorization.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster that has changed monetization partners or retired channels over time, the ads.txt and app-ads.txt files can carry lines from arrangements that have since ended. You may want to reconcile the seller lines against the partners and FreeWheel accounts in use today, and remove the lines tied to channels or relationships that no longer exist.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce brand whose video monetization through FreeWheel may have started as a test or a single campaign, the file can hold lines added during that earlier setup. You may want to confirm that each remaining line reflects a seller and account the brand still uses for the video inventory it monetizes now.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's current list of seller account IDs assigned to the publisher, used to reconcile lines against active relationships, is available in the FreeWheel partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Read every seller line in the publisher's ads.txt and app-ads.txt files and reconcile each one against the current list of authorized sellers and FreeWheel accounts.

browser.label: Web browser
steps: Open the publisher's ads.txt and each app-ads.txt and list every seller line, recording the seller domain, account ID, and relationship for each.
Mark each line as current, stale, duplicate, or unrecognized by comparing it against the publisher's current list of authorized sellers and FreeWheel accounts.
Flag any FreeWheel account ID that appears on more than one line, noting whether the relationship differs between the duplicates.
Record every line that cannot be tied to a current authorized relationship so it can be reviewed for removal.

ads_txt_validator.label: ads.txt and app-ads.txt validator
steps: Run each file through a public ads.txt validator and note any duplicate or malformed lines it reports.
Use the validator's parsed line list to cross-check the manual reconciliation since the parser surfaces repeated entries that are easy to miss by eye.
Record the duplicate and malformed lines the validator flags alongside the stale and unrecognized lines found by reconciliation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every seller line in the ads.txt and app-ads.txt files and reconciles each against the current list of authorized sellers and FreeWheel accounts. A file where every line ties to a current authorized relationship, with no duplicates and no unrecognized accounts, passes. A file holding stale lines from ended relationships, duplicate FreeWheel entries, or lines the publisher cannot tie to a current authorization is a fail recorded with each line flagged for removal or review.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each seller line and its reconciliation status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt or app-ads.txt for {{site_name}} holds stale, duplicate, or unrecognized seller lines that no longer reflect current authorized relationships. Because buyers read the file as the authoritative list of who may sell the inventory, a line left in place after a relationship ends keeps a path open for inventory to be sold under {{site_name}}'s name, and a cluttered file is harder to verify. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling every seller line against the current list of authorized sellers and FreeWheel accounts, then removing the stale, duplicate, and unrecognized lines so the file states only current authorizations. After the cleanup, run each file through a validator to confirm it parses cleanly.
